title: The assembly and fate of a giant disc galaxy in a protocluster at $z = 3$
comment: 24 pages, 14 figures. Submitted to MNRAS; comments are welcome
summary: Recent JWST observations revealed two massive ($M_{\star} \gtrsim 10^{11}\,M_{\odot}$), unexpectedly large spiral galaxies at $z \sim 3$, both in overdense environments. We focus on one of these, ADF22.1 at $z = 3.09$, which hosts an active galactic nucleus (AGN), exploiting its extended [CII] emission ($\sim$30 kpc in diameter) with high-resolution observations from the Atacama Large Millimetre Array and JWST. We find a flat outer rotation curve reaching $\sim$530 km s$^{-1}$, and perform, for the first time for a system of this type, a rotation-curve decomposition. We infer a dark-matter halo mass of $\log(M_{200}/M_{\odot})=12.9^{+0.4}_{-0.3}$, a baryon-to-halo mass ratio of $0.4^{+0.6}_{-0.3}$ in units of the cosmological baryon fraction, and a ratio between the baryonic and dark-matter halo specific angular momentum of $1.0^{+0.7}_{-0.5}$. Comparing these quantities with those of local galaxies, we find that ADF22.1 is indistinguishable from $z=0$ giant discs, pointing to the inefficiency of AGN feedback in halting disc growth. Using the Mapping Nearby Galaxies at Apache Point Observatory survey, we identify potential $z=0$ descendants of ADF22.1, suggesting it will evolve into an extreme (in either mass or angular momentum) early-type galaxy. Finally, we argue that cold-stream accretion, invoked to explain disc formation at $z > 1$, cannot simultaneously account for its size, dynamical properties, high specific angular momentum, and baryon-to-halo mass ratio. Instead, sustained accretion from the hot circumgalactic medium, either via spontaneous or fountain-driven condensation, offers a more physically plausible formation pathway.

AI.tldr: JWST reveals massive spiral galaxy ADF22.1 at z=3.09 with extended [CII] emission; rotation curve decomposition shows dark matter halo mass ~10^12.9 M⊙, high baryon fraction, and angular momentum similar to local giant discs, suggesting AGN feedback inefficiency and hot CGM accretion formation pathway.
AI.motivation: To understand the formation and evolution of massive, large spiral galaxies at high redshift (z~3) discovered by JWST, particularly ADF22.1 with AGN activity, by analyzing its dynamical properties, dark matter halo, baryon content, and angular momentum to constrain formation mechanisms.
AI.method: Utilized high-resolution ALMA observations of extended [CII] emission (~30 kpc diameter) and JWST data for ADF22.1 at z=3.09; performed rotation curve decomposition to separate baryonic and dark matter components; compared with local galaxies using MaNGA survey; analyzed dynamical properties including rotation velocity, halo mass, baryon fraction, and specific angular momentum.
AI.result: Found flat outer rotation curve reaching ~530 km/s; dark matter halo mass log(M200/M⊙)=12.9^{+0.4}_{-0.3}; baryon-to-halo mass ratio of 0.4^{+0.6}_{-0.3} times cosmological fraction; baryonic to dark matter specific angular momentum ratio of 1.0^{+0.7}_{-0.5}; galaxy indistinguishable from z=0 giant discs; potential descendants identified as extreme early-type galaxies.
AI.conclusion: ADF22.1's properties suggest AGN feedback is inefficient at halting disc growth; cold-stream accretion cannot explain its size, dynamics, angular momentum, and baryon fraction; sustained accretion from hot circumgalactic medium via condensation offers more plausible formation pathway for such massive high-z spirals.

title: How Robust is the Cosmic Distance with Tip of Red Giant Branch against Stellar Population Variations?
comment: Accepted for publication in ApJ, 15 pages, 11 figures, 4 tables
summary: The tip of the red giant branch (TRGB) provides a key standard candle for extragalactic distance measurements and for refining the Hubble constant. We test its robustness by quantifying how metallicity, $α$-element enhancement, age, and initial helium abundance modulate the TRGB luminosity, using synthetic composite color--magnitude diagrams in the $I$ and $F814W$ bands. We find that metallicity and $α$-element enhancement are the primary drivers of TRGB variation, while age introduces only a modest effect and helium abundance is negligible. At fixed age and helium content, increasing the mean metallicity by 0.5 dex or the $α$-element enhancement by 0.3 dex produces the well-known systematic dimming of 0.046 and 0.050 mag, respectively, in $M_I^{\rm TRGB}$, and of 0.093 and 0.044 mag, respectively, in $M_{F814W}^{\rm TRGB}$. By comparison, changes in age of 3~Gyr and in initial helium abundance of 0.10 yield minor luminosity shifts, with average changes of 0.031 and 0.009~mag, respectively, in $M_I^{\rm TRGB}$, and of 0.035 and 0.027 mag, respectively, in $M_{F814W}^{\rm TRGB}$, substantially smaller than those caused by variations in metallicity or $α$-element enhancement. For mixed stellar populations under typical stellar-halo metallicity conditions, the net variation in $M_I^{\rm TRGB}$ arising from each combination of the $α$-element enhancement, age, and initial helium abundance remains below 0.028~mag, well within reported systematic uncertainties. Together, these results reaffirm the TRGB as a highly robust distance indicator and support its continued use as an independent anchor for precision cosmology in the era of the Hubble-tension debate.

AI.tldr: The TRGB luminosity is primarily affected by metallicity and α-element enhancement, with age and initial helium abundance having minor effects, confirming TRGB's robustness as a distance indicator.
AI.motivation: To test the robustness of the tip of the red giant branch (TRGB) as a standard candle for extragalactic distance measurements and Hubble constant refinement by quantifying how stellar population parameters affect its luminosity.
AI.method: Using synthetic composite color-magnitude diagrams in I and F814W bands to quantify how metallicity, α-element enhancement, age, and initial helium abundance modulate TRGB luminosity.
AI.result: Metallicity and α-element enhancement are primary drivers of TRGB variation (0.5 dex metallicity increase causes 0.046 mag dimming in M_I; 0.3 dex α-enhancement causes 0.050 mag dimming). Age and helium have minor effects (3 Gyr age change yields 0.031 mag shift; 0.10 helium change yields 0.009 mag shift). For mixed populations under typical halo conditions, net variation remains below 0.028 mag.
AI.conclusion: TRGB is a highly robust distance indicator with variations well within reported systematic uncertainties, supporting its continued use as an independent anchor for precision cosmology in the Hubble-tension era.

title: Weak-lensing Analysis of Intracluster Filaments in Abell 2744: Matched-filter Scans and Stepwise 2D Tracing
comment: 20 pages, 13 figures; submitted to ApJ
summary: We present a weak-lensing (WL) analysis of filamentary structures in the merging galaxy cluster Abell 2744 using wide-field Magellan/MegaCam imaging data. We employ two complementary techniques: standard matched-filter scans to identify global orientations, and a new stepwise 2D tracing method to reconstruct locally varying filament orientations. The matched-filter analysis detects coherent filamentary features in the northwest and east directions across both inner (1.0-2.2 Mpc) and outer (2.2-3.4 Mpc) annuli. However, while the northwest filament yields consistent constraints across both regions, parameter inference for the eastern structure remains unstable and radially inconsistent when restricted to global reference-point scans. We demonstrate that re-characterizing the eastern structure using the locally preferred elongation directions from our stepwise tracer significantly resolves these tensions, improving fit quality and bringing inner and outer constraints into agreement. Furthermore, the detected filaments align well with diffuse X-ray structures and previously identified merger axes, supporting their physical connection to the cluster's mass assembly. These results highlight that stepwise 2D tracing is essential for characterizing curved or complex filaments where global reference-point scans are insufficient.

AI.tldr: Weak-lensing analysis of Abell 2744 reveals filamentary structures using matched-filter scans and a new stepwise 2D tracing method, showing improved characterization of complex filaments compared to global methods.
AI.motivation: To analyze filamentary structures in the merging galaxy cluster Abell 2744 using weak-lensing techniques, addressing limitations of global orientation methods for complex filament characterization.
AI.method: Employed two complementary weak-lensing techniques: standard matched-filter scans for global orientation identification, and a new stepwise 2D tracing method for reconstructing locally varying filament orientations using wide-field Magellan/MegaCam imaging data.
AI.result: Detected coherent filamentary features in northwest and east directions across inner (1.0-2.2 Mpc) and outer (2.2-3.4 Mpc) annuli. The northwest filament showed consistent constraints, while eastern structure exhibited unstable parameter inference with global scans. Stepwise tracing resolved tensions, improved fit quality, and brought inner/outer constraints into agreement. Filaments aligned with diffuse X-ray structures and known merger axes.
AI.conclusion: Stepwise 2D tracing is essential for characterizing curved or complex filaments where global reference-point scans are insufficient, demonstrating its importance for accurate filament reconstruction in merging galaxy clusters.

title: The Structure of Molecular Gas in PHANGS-ALMA Galaxies: Cloud Spacing, Two-Point Correlation and Stacked Intensity Profiles
comment: 18 pages main text, 5 pages appendix, 13 figures, accepted to A&A
summary: The sub-kpc scale gas structure encodes key information of giant molecular cloud (GMC) formation. Therefore, we aim for a quantitative description of molecular gas structure across 150-1000 pc using a sample of 8984 GMCs from 40 galaxies observed by PHANGS-ALMA. We homogenize our data to a fixed resolution of 150 pc and mass sensitivity of 2.5 M$_{\odot}$ pc$^{-2}$ to remove observational bias. We then calculate nearest neighbour distances, neighbour number density, and two-point correlation functions for the catalogued GMCs. When analysing the two-point correlation function, we generate several control samples that reflect different null hypotheses on large spatial scales. We stack integrated intensity CO emission profiles around the position of catalogued GMCs to probe the gas distribution on scales between the resolution limit and the typical GMC-GMC spacing. Our measurements of cloud spacing and number of neighbours show that GMC clustering follows the large-scale gas distribution. Once we account for this contribution, the peak excess clustering in the two-point correlation function drops from 1+$ω$ of 2.3 to 1.3, with the power-law slope flattened from -0.25 to 0. We show that the stacked CO intensity profiles around CO peaks can be recovered by the "GMC size" measured by CPROPS, with an additional 20% of the flux in an extended component beyond 500 pc. We find that our stacked profiles can be fit with a double Gaussian function plus a constant offset. The broad Gaussian component accounts for 70% of the over-density power above the constant offset, and is stronger around massive and gravitationally bound GMCs. Our results indicate that galactic structure regulates the GMC distribution in galaxy disks, and the formation of massive, gravitationally bound GMCs is related to strong local gas clustering.

AI.tldr: PHANGS-ALMA study of 8984 GMCs across 40 galaxies reveals that galactic structure regulates GMC distribution, with massive bound GMCs forming in regions of strong local gas clustering.
AI.motivation: To quantitatively describe molecular gas structure at sub-kpc scales (150-1000 pc) to understand giant molecular cloud (GMC) formation, using the statistical properties of GMC distribution across multiple galaxies.
AI.method: Analyzed 8984 GMCs from 40 PHANGS-ALMA galaxies with homogenized 150 pc resolution and 2.5 M⊙ pc⁻² sensitivity. Calculated nearest neighbor distances, neighbor number density, and two-point correlation functions with control samples. Stacked CO intensity profiles around GMC positions to probe gas distribution between resolution limit and typical GMC spacing.
AI.result: GMC clustering follows large-scale gas distribution. After accounting for this, peak excess clustering drops from 1+ω of 2.3 to 1.3 with power-law slope flattening from -0.25 to 0. Stacked CO profiles recover CPROPS-measured GMC sizes plus 20% extended flux beyond 500 pc. Profiles fit with double Gaussian plus constant offset, with broad component accounting for 70% of overdensity power, stronger around massive bound GMCs.
AI.conclusion: Galactic structure regulates GMC distribution in galaxy disks, and formation of massive, gravitationally bound GMCs is related to strong local gas clustering.

title: Searching for GEMS: Discovery of the Nearby Post-Common-Envelope Binary System TIC-460388167
summary: Short-period white dwarf+main-sequence binaries are Post-Common-Envelope Binaries (PCEB) that have survived a common envelope phase. Such systems, if detached and eclipsing, enable precise measurements of the constituent stars, providing a unique opportunity to probe the effects of the common envelope phase on the system. We report the discovery of one such nearby (57 pc) system, TIC-460388167, using a combination of multi-band photometric light curves and spectroscopic radial velocities. In addition to eclipses, the system exhibits a continuously variable light curve that we model as a combination of ellipsoidal variations and star spots. We determine a period $P$=0.63596258$\pm$0.00000012 d and inclination $i$=89.0$\pm$0.4 deg. The best-fitting model specifies a white dwarf with T$_1$=7607$\pm$127 K and radius R$_1$=0.0131$\pm$0.0003 $R_\odot$, which is eclipsed by a T$_2$=3151 $\pm$ 59 K, R$_2$=0.327$\pm$0.006 $R_\odot$ M dwarf. The white dwarf mass is 0.61$\pm$0.04 M$_\odot$. We present the first velocity resolved profile for a PCEB secondary and show that the rotation of the M-dwarf is synchronous with the orbital period, as expected. We compare the constituent stars to other PCEB systems and find TIC-460388167A is one of the coolest known white dwarfs in such systems. TIC-460388167 is among the longest period eclipsing PCEB systems known.

AI.tldr: Discovery and characterization of TIC-460388167, a nearby eclipsing post-common-envelope binary system consisting of a cool white dwarf and an M dwarf with synchronous rotation.
AI.motivation: To study the effects of common envelope evolution by discovering and characterizing detached, eclipsing post-common-envelope binaries that enable precise measurements of stellar parameters.
AI.method: Combined multi-band photometric light curves and spectroscopic radial velocities to discover and model TIC-460388167, analyzing eclipses, ellipsoidal variations, and star spots to determine system parameters.
AI.result: Identified a 57 pc system with period 0.63596258 d, inclination 89.0°, containing a 7607 K, 0.0131 R⊙ white dwarf (0.61 M⊙) and a 3151 K, 0.327 R⊙ M dwarf; demonstrated synchronous rotation of the secondary.
AI.conclusion: TIC-460388167 represents one of the coolest known white dwarfs in PCEB systems and among the longest period eclipsing PCEBs, providing valuable constraints for common envelope evolution models.

title: Tracing Active Galactic Nuclei Properties Through a Changing-look Event
comment: 13 pages, 5 figures, accepted for publication in MNRAS
summary: Changing-look transitions challenge our understanding of active galactic nuclei (AGN), exhibiting dramatic changes in broad-line emission and continuum flux on timescales of months to years. We present a detailed study of the spectroscopically confirmed changing-look AGN ZTF18abuamgo. Combining photometric survey data with spectroscopy spanning three epochs over 20 years, we identify a turn-on transition from a Type 1.5 to Type 1.2 AGN and estimate the timescale of this change to be as short as four years. Spectral analysis indicates that this transformation is driven by a rapid increase in accretion rate, with the Eddington ratio rising from $0.032 \pm 0.005$ in the dim state to $0.08 \pm 0.01$ in the bright state. For the first time in a changing-look AGN, we apply the Boltzmann plot method to the visible Balmer series emission, deriving broad line region electron temperatures of $11,800 \pm 900$ K and $11,900 \pm 2,400$ K in 2022 and 2024, respectively. Applying single-epoch black hole mass estimation to the brightening H$α$ emission, we find a mass of $(5.0 \pm 0.4) \times 10^7 M_\odot$. The consistency in this estimate across all spectroscopic epochs suggest that even highly variable broad lines in CL-AGN do not bias the results derived using this method. Our results demonstrate that objects like ZTF18abuamgo provide a unique laboratory to study extreme AGN variability, probe the physical conditions in the broad line region, and assess the limitations of widely used black hole mass estimation methods.

AI.tldr: Study of changing-look AGN ZTF18abuamgo shows rapid transition from Type 1.5 to Type 1.2 AGN in ~4 years, driven by increased accretion rate, with consistent black hole mass estimates despite extreme variability.
AI.motivation: Changing-look AGN transitions challenge AGN understanding, exhibiting dramatic spectral changes on short timescales. Studying these objects provides insights into extreme AGN variability, broad line region physics, and validation of black hole mass estimation methods.
AI.method: Combined photometric survey data with spectroscopy spanning three epochs over 20 years. Applied Boltzmann plot method to visible Balmer series emission to derive broad line region electron temperatures. Used single-epoch black hole mass estimation on Hα emission. Analyzed spectral changes and accretion rate variations.
AI.result: Identified turn-on transition from Type 1.5 to Type 1.2 AGN in ~4 years. Eddington ratio increased from 0.032±0.005 to 0.08±0.01. Derived BLR electron temperatures of 11,800±900 K (2022) and 11,900±2,400 K (2024). Estimated black hole mass of (5.0±0.4)×10⁷ M⊙, consistent across epochs despite variability.
AI.conclusion: Changing-look AGN like ZTF18abuamgo serve as unique laboratories for studying extreme AGN variability, probing BLR physical conditions, and validating black hole mass estimation methods. Results show that even highly variable broad lines in CL-AGN do not bias single-epoch mass estimates.

title: Solar Extreme Ultraviolet Spectrograph and High-energy Imager (SEUSHI): Design, Development, and Pre-Flight Calibration
comment: 23 pages, 14 figures, 3 tables
summary: Understanding the initiation of solar flares and coronal mass ejections (CMEs) is essential for improving forecasts of extreme space weather. Soft X-ray (SXR) and Extreme Ultraviolet (EUV) observations provide critical diagnostics of the highly dynamic solar corona; however, significant measurement gaps persist despite decades of observations. The Solar Extreme Ultraviolet Spectrograph and High-energy Imager (SEUSHI) aims to address these gaps by combining multi-pinhole SXR imaging with grazing-incidence EUV spectroscopy on a shared camera. SEUSHI delivers spatially-resolved temperature and emission measure maps at 1 arcminute resolution and 5 second cadence to identify Hot Onset Precursor Events (HOPEs), which provide early alerts of flares. Additionally, high-cadence (100 Hz) readouts of selected image rows enable photon-counting spectroscopy over 1.1-6.8 keV with approx. 0.08 keV energy resolution, to investigate elemental abundance evolution in active regions, a key diagnostic of coronal heating. SEUSHI also provides high-resolution (approx. 0.2 nm) EUV spectra measurements across the 16.1-33.8 nm range at 5 second cadence for studies of coronal dimming and generation of early alerts for CMEs. SEUSHI is designed with low power, mass, and volume requirements, making it suitable for small satellite platforms. A technology demonstration version of SEUSHI is currently under development for flight aboard the Solar Dynamics Observatory Extreme Ultraviolet Variability Experiment calibration sounding rocket. This paper presents the instrument design, development, and calibration. Successful demonstration on the sounding rocket platform is an important step towards the opportunity to fly SEUSHI on future satellite missions and thus to improve space weather operations.

AI.tldr: SEUSHI is a novel solar instrument combining multi-pinhole SXR imaging with grazing-incidence EUV spectroscopy to address measurement gaps in solar flare and CME observations, designed for small satellite platforms with a sounding rocket demonstration underway.
AI.motivation: To address persistent measurement gaps in SXR and EUV observations of the solar corona despite decades of observations, which are critical for understanding solar flare and CME initiation and improving space weather forecasting.
AI.method: Combines multi-pinhole SXR imaging with grazing-incidence EUV spectroscopy on a shared camera, providing spatially-resolved temperature/emission measure maps at 1 arcmin resolution/5s cadence, photon-counting spectroscopy over 1.1-6.8 keV with 0.08 keV resolution, and high-resolution EUV spectra across 16.1-33.8 nm at 5s cadence.
AI.result: Instrument design enables identification of Hot Onset Precursor Events (HOPEs) for early flare alerts, investigation of elemental abundance evolution in active regions, and studies of coronal dimming for CME early alerts, with low power/mass/volume requirements suitable for small satellites.
AI.conclusion: SEUSHI represents an important advancement in solar observation capabilities, with its sounding rocket demonstration being a critical step toward future satellite deployment to enhance space weather operations through improved flare and CME forecasting.

title: Two young open clusters in Cygnus and their vicinity: combining multicolor photometry with Gaia DR3 astrometry
comment: 19 pages, 14 figures
summary:
We investigate two neighboring clusters in the Cygnus complex, Berkeley 86 and Berkeley 87, with a primary emphasis on the evaluation of extinction in the field of view towards and across the clusters. We also analyze their kinematic behavior in space and time to discern their possible common origin and relation to the Cyg~OB1 association.
  New CCD photometry in the Vilnius seven-color system, obtained down to V=19.0 mag in the fields of these two clusters, is used to classify stars in terms of spectral and luminosity classes and to determine the individual values of interstellar extinction. The probable cluster members are identified in a 5-parameter space based on Gaia DR3. The cluster ages and stellar masses are derived through the use of the HR diagrams. To obtain the 3D kinematics of the clusters and trace their orbits back in time, we combine the Gaia-based proper-motions and distances with radial velocities from the literature.
  The estimated cluster properties show that both clusters are almost equidistant (1.7 kpc) and nearly coeval, with average ages of 6.1$\pm$0.5 and 6.5$\pm$0.4 Myr, respectively, and age dispersion of 3 Myr. The nonuniformity of extinction is evident within each cluster, especially pronounced across the face of Berkeley 86 where the most-massive stars show substantial substructure. By extrapolating the observed mass function to a minimum stellar mass, we obtain cluster masses of 519 M(Sun) and 1551 M(Sun) for Berkeley 86 and 87, respectively. Although both clusters share very similar properties, their orbital paths show no indication that they had a common birthplace, however Berkeley 87 and its neighbor NGC 6913 are very likely to have been born in pair.

AI.tldr: Study of two Cygnus complex clusters (Berkeley 86 & 87) using photometry, Gaia data, and kinematics to determine extinction patterns, cluster properties, and investigate possible common origin.
AI.motivation: To evaluate interstellar extinction patterns across two neighboring clusters in the Cygnus complex and analyze their kinematic behavior to discern possible common origin and relation to the Cyg OB1 association.
AI.method: Used new CCD photometry in Vilnius seven-color system (down to V=19.0 mag) for spectral classification and extinction determination. Identified cluster members using Gaia DR3 5-parameter space. Derived ages and masses via HR diagrams. Combined Gaia proper motions, distances with literature radial velocities for 3D kinematics and orbit tracing.
AI.result: Both clusters nearly equidistant (1.7 kpc) and coeval (6.1±0.5 Myr for Berkeley 86, 6.5±0.4 Myr for Berkeley 87) with 3 Myr age dispersion. Nonuniform extinction evident, especially across Berkeley 86. Cluster masses: 519 M☉ (Berkeley 86) and 1551 M☉ (Berkeley 87). Orbital paths show no common birthplace for Berkeley 86 & 87, but Berkeley 87 and NGC 6913 likely formed as a pair.
AI.conclusion: Despite sharing similar properties (distance, age), Berkeley 86 and 87 did not originate from a common birthplace based on orbital analysis. However, Berkeley 87 and NGC 6913 appear to be a likely binary cluster system born together.

title: Stochastic Optical Variability and an rms-flux Relation in the Intermediate Polar EP240309a
comment: 12 pages, 4 figures, 2 tables. Accepted for publication in ApJS
summary: Magnetic cataclysmic variables provide a natural laboratory for studying how accretion interacts with compact-object magnetospheres and generates stochastic variability. We present an optical variability study of the intermediate-polar candidate EP240309a, an Einstein Probe X-ray transient, using BOOTES photometry, high-cadence TESS light curves, and a SOAR/Goodman optical spectrum. Previous studies found a white-dwarf spin period of 3.97 min (Pspin ~ 238 s) and an orbital period of Porb = 3.7614(4) h. Power spectral densities from the BOOTES data are consistent with single power laws with slopes alpha ~ 1.2-1.8, with no statistically significant evidence for a bend across the sampled frequency range. Using red-noise simulations and injection-recovery tests, we place one-sided constraints on any putative break frequency, which translate, under standard dynamical identifications, into an upper limit on the magnetospheric radius of Rm <= few x 10^10 cm for MWD = 0.8 Msun. In the TESS data, we detect a linear rms-flux relation on hour timescales in three high-cadence sectors, while two other sectors do not show a robust detection, indicating epoch-dependent rms-flux behavior. The SOAR spectrum shows Balmer and He II emission lines with FWHM about 1000-1600 km s^-1; under a Keplerian interpretation, these imply characteristic radii of r about (0.9-3.4) x 10^10 cm, broadly comparable to the timing-based constraints. Overall, the data provide conservative, order-of-magnitude radius constraints consistent with accretion onto a magnetic white dwarf, but they do not establish the detailed accretion geometry or exclude stream-fed or mixed accretion scenarios.

AI.tldr: Optical variability study of magnetic cataclysmic variable EP240309a reveals power-law noise, rms-flux relation, and emission lines, providing order-of-magnitude constraints on magnetospheric radius (~10^10 cm) consistent with accretion onto a magnetic white dwarf.
AI.motivation: Magnetic cataclysmic variables serve as natural laboratories for studying accretion physics and magnetosphere interactions, particularly the stochastic variability generated during accretion onto compact objects with strong magnetic fields.
AI.method: Multi-wavelength observational approach using BOOTES photometry for power spectral analysis, TESS high-cadence light curves for rms-flux relation study, and SOAR/Goodman optical spectroscopy for emission line analysis, supplemented by red-noise simulations and injection-recovery tests.
AI.result: Power spectra show single power laws (α ~ 1.2-1.8) without significant breaks, constraining magnetospheric radius to Rm ≤ few × 10^10 cm. TESS data reveal epoch-dependent rms-flux relations on hour timescales. Emission lines (FWHM ~ 1000-1600 km/s) imply characteristic radii ~(0.9-3.4) × 10^10 cm, consistent with timing constraints.
AI.conclusion: The data provide conservative, order-of-magnitude radius constraints supporting accretion onto a magnetic white dwarf, but cannot establish detailed accretion geometry or discriminate between stream-fed and mixed accretion scenarios.

title: Multiphase Gas Structure in the Circumnuclear Region of NGC 5506 Observed with ALMA
comment: 16 pages, 11 figures, accepted for publication in ApJ
summary: We present a study of the multiphase gas structure and kinematics of the circumnuclear disk (CND) of NGC 5506, a nearby edge-on Seyfert galaxy, at a spatial resolution of $\sim20$ pc. Observations of [C I](1-0), CO(3-2), and HCO$^{+}$(4-3) obtained with the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array reveal the CND dominated by rotational motion on scales of several hundred parsecs. No significant differences in geometrical thickness or velocity structure are found between [C I](1-0) and CO(3-2) across the CND, whereas HCO$^{+}$(4-3) emission is more concentrated toward the disk plane. The ratio of velocity dispersion to rotational velocity, a proxy for disk scale height-to-radius ratio, is high ($\gtrsim0.9$) in the central region ($\lesssim30$ pc) for both [C I](1-0) and CO(3-2), indicating geometrically thick structures in both tracers. Regions where the [C I](1-0)/CO(3-2) ratio exceeds the CND average are spatially correlated with the [O III]$λ$5007 bicone observed with the Hubble Space Telescope, suggesting that CO is preferentially dissociated by the AGN-driven biconical ionized outflow. The observed CND scale height and velocity dispersions traced by [C I](1-0) and CO(3-2) are consistent with a model in which supernova-driven turbulence provides the vertical support for the CND.

AI.tldr: ALMA observations of NGC 5506's circumnuclear disk reveal thick molecular structures supported by supernova-driven turbulence, with CO dissociation by AGN outflow indicated by [C I]/CO ratio enhancements correlated with [O III] bicone.
AI.motivation: To investigate the multiphase gas structure and kinematics of the circumnuclear disk in NGC 5506 at high spatial resolution (~20 pc), examining how different molecular tracers ([C I], CO, HCO⁺) reveal gas properties and their relation to AGN feedback processes.
AI.method: ALMA observations of [C I](1-0), CO(3-2), and HCO⁺(4-3) emission lines at ~20 pc spatial resolution, combined with HST [O III]λ5007 imaging. Analysis includes comparing geometrical thickness, velocity structure, velocity dispersion-to-rotational velocity ratios, and spatial correlation between [C I]/CO ratios and AGN outflow features.
AI.result: CND dominated by rotational motion; no significant differences between [C I] and CO in thickness/velocity structure; HCO⁺ more concentrated toward disk plane; high velocity dispersion-to-rotational velocity ratio (≳0.9) in central region indicates geometrically thick structures; enhanced [C I]/CO ratios spatially correlated with [O III] bicone; observed scale heights and velocity dispersions consistent with supernova-driven turbulence providing vertical support.
AI.conclusion: The circumnuclear disk of NGC 5506 exhibits geometrically thick molecular structures supported by supernova-driven turbulence. The spatial correlation between enhanced [C I]/CO ratios and the AGN-driven ionized outflow suggests CO dissociation by the AGN biconical outflow, providing evidence for AGN feedback affecting molecular gas chemistry in the CND.

title: The ZTF-ULTRASAT experiment: Characterizing the non-transients in ULTRASAT's high cadence survey
comment: 12 pages,13 figures
summary: The forthcoming launch of the Ultraviolet Transient Astronomy Satellite (ULTRASAT) will transform our understanding of the transient ultraviolet sky by increasing our ability to identify transients due to its unprecedented 204 deg2 field of view. While rapid (extragalactic) transients are a priority science area for the mission, flaring stars and AGN can often contaminate searches for such objects. To prepare for these challenges, the Zwicky Transient Facility (ZTF)-ULTRASAT experiment observed five fields at high cadence over three nights, in close proximity to ULTRASAT's three northern high-cadence fields. A real-time filter identified seven transient candidates, of which five were presistent variable sources and two were spurious. Periods and amplitudes derived from the ZRF Source Classifcation Project (SCoPe) showed that three candidates were RR Lyrae stars with short periods and high amplitudes, while the remaining two displayed flairing behavior. We demonstrate that short-timescale, high-amplitude variables can sysematically mimic transient alerts in high-cadence UV surveys, and we provide a concrete startegy to this contamination using pre-existing machine learning catalogs.

AI.tldr: ZTF-ULTRASAT experiment identifies stellar variables (RR Lyrae stars and flaring sources) that contaminate transient searches in upcoming ULTRASAT UV surveys, proposing ML-based filtering strategies.
AI.motivation: To prepare for contamination challenges in ULTRASAT's transient searches, particularly from flaring stars and AGN that can mimic rapid extragalactic transients in UV surveys.
AI.method: Conducted ZTF-ULTRASAT experiment observing five fields at high cadence over three nights near ULTRASAT's northern high-cadence fields, using real-time filtering to identify transient candidates, then analyzing periods and amplitudes via ZTF Source Classification Project (SCoPe).
AI.result: Identified seven transient candidates: five persistent variable sources (three RR Lyrae stars with short periods/high amplitudes, two flaring sources) and two spurious detections, demonstrating systematic contamination by short-timescale, high-amplitude variables.
AI.conclusion: Short-timescale, high-amplitude variables systematically contaminate transient alerts in high-cadence UV surveys; proposes using pre-existing machine learning catalogs as a concrete strategy to mitigate this contamination for ULTRASAT.

title: Candidate Microlensing Brown Dwarfs in Binary Lens Systems from the 2023--2025 Observing Seasons
comment: 17 pages, 12 figures
summary: We present detailed light-curve analyses of ten binary-lens microlensing events observed during the 2023--2025 seasons and selected as candidates for hosting brown-dwarf companions. The sample includes OGLE-2023-BLG-0249, KMT-2023-BLG-1246, OGLE-2023-BLG-0079, KMT-2024-BLG-0072, KMT-2024-BLG-0897, KMT-2024-BLG-1876, KMT-2024-BLG-2379, KMT-2025-BLG-0922, KMT-2025-BLG-1056, and KMT-2025-BLG-2427. For each event, we carry out modeling of the light curve, explore relevant degeneracies, and, when finite-source effects are present, determine the angular Einstein radius. For OGLE-2023-BLG-0249, we additionally measure the microlens parallax, which allows a direct determination of the lens masses and distance. For the remaining events, we estimate the physical lens properties via Bayesian analyses incorporating Galactic priors. The resulting posteriors show that the lens companions in all systems have median masses in the brown-dwarf regime, and the lenses of two events (KMT-2025-BLG-0922 and KMT-2025-BLG-1056) are consistent with binaries in which both lens components fall within the brown-dwarf mass range. Spanning a wide range of projected separations and distances, these detections illustrate the power of high-cadence microlensing surveys to build a census of brown-dwarf companions, including faint and distant systems beyond the reach of flux-limited methods.

AI.tldr: Analysis of 10 binary-lens microlensing events from 2023-2025 reveals brown-dwarf companions, including two binary brown-dwarf systems, demonstrating microlensing's capability to detect faint, distant brown dwarfs beyond flux-limited methods.
AI.motivation: To build a census of brown-dwarf companions through microlensing observations, particularly targeting faint and distant systems that are inaccessible to flux-limited detection methods, and to characterize their physical properties.
AI.method: Detailed light-curve analysis of 10 binary-lens microlensing events observed during 2023-2025 seasons. For each event: modeling of light curves, exploration of degeneracies, determination of angular Einstein radius when finite-source effects are present. For OGLE-2023-BLG-0249: measurement of microlens parallax for direct mass/distance determination. For remaining events: Bayesian analyses with Galactic priors to estimate physical lens properties.
AI.result: All 10 systems have lens companions with median masses in the brown-dwarf regime. Two events (KMT-2025-BLG-0922 and KMT-2025-BLG-1056) show both lens components consistent with brown-dwarf masses, representing binary brown-dwarf systems. The sample spans wide ranges of projected separations and distances.
AI.conclusion: High-cadence microlensing surveys are powerful tools for building a comprehensive census of brown-dwarf companions, capable of detecting faint and distant systems that are beyond the reach of flux-limited detection methods, as demonstrated by this sample of 10 events including binary brown-dwarf systems.

title: The Fate of Globular Cluster Substructure: A Kinematic Response to Galaxy Assembly
comment: 21 pages, 11 figures, accepted for publication in MNRAS
summary: Globular clusters (GCs) are powerful tracers of galaxy assembly, frequently used to identify accreted substructure and reconstruct hierarchical merger histories. With advances in GC formation models and cosmological simulations, we can now better quantify the information about galaxy evolution encoded in present-day GCs. Here, we investigate how GC kinematics evolve over cosmic time and assess the extent to which GCs retain memory of the past of their host galaxy. Using a GC formation model applied to five Milky Way (MW) analogues from the Latte suite of the FIRE-2 simulations, we track the evolution of kinematic properties. At $z=0$, in-situ and ex-situ GCs exhibit substantial overlap in kinematic space, indicating that these populations are not clearly separable. We find that a subset of kinematic properties evolve in an ordered fashion across both in-situ and ex-situ populations, whereas others are dominated by stochastic variations. As a result, by the present day, most memory of the progenitor of an accreted GC is erased and only a few correlations persist. These correlations link progenitor halo mass to the total mass and number of a GC population, and the galactocentric distance of GC substructure to progenitor maximum circular velocity. These results highlight how both deterministic and stochastic processes driven by galaxy evolution shape GC kinematics and demonstrate the limits of reconstructing the assembly history of a galaxy from present-day GC orbits alone.

AI.tldr: GC kinematics evolve through both deterministic and stochastic processes, erasing most memory of progenitor origins by present day, limiting reconstruction of galaxy assembly history from orbits alone.
AI.motivation: To understand how globular cluster kinematics evolve over cosmic time and assess how much information about galaxy assembly history is preserved in present-day GC orbits, particularly for distinguishing in-situ vs ex-situ populations.
AI.method: Used GC formation model applied to five Milky Way analogues from the Latte suite of FIRE-2 cosmological simulations, tracking kinematic property evolution over cosmic time.
AI.result: At z=0, in-situ and ex-situ GCs show substantial kinematic overlap, making them difficult to separate. Most progenitor memory is erased by present day, with only a few correlations persisting: progenitor halo mass correlates with GC population total mass/number, and progenitor maximum circular velocity correlates with GC substructure galactocentric distance.
AI.conclusion: Both deterministic and stochastic evolutionary processes shape GC kinematics, erasing most memory of progenitor origins. This demonstrates limitations in reconstructing galaxy assembly history from present-day GC orbits alone, despite GCs being valuable tracers of galaxy evolution.

title: eROSITA's cool star population explained
comment: Accepted by Astronomy & Astrophysics
summary: The rotation-activity connection is the standard paradigm for interpreting chromospheric and coronal activity in late-type stars, namely, stars with outer convection zones. This paradigm states that activity increases with decreasing rotation period until a saturation limit is reached. By scaling rotation periods with the convective turnover time via the Rossby number, $\text{Ro}$, saturation is expected to occur at a universal value across all spectral types. In our paper, we systematically investigate the relationship between rotation and activity as measured though X-ray emission for a large sample of late-type stars to test the universal applicability of this paradigm. To this end, we utilized TESS short-cadence space photometry to determine the rotation periods for late-type stars identified in the eROSITA all-sky survey. This combined dataset provides rotation and X-ray measurements for 14004 stars, representing a sample size increase of more than an order of magnitude compared to previous studies. We find that the convective turnover times derived from this sample closely agree with theoretical computations, supporting the idea that Rossby number-activity relations hold for all late-type main sequence stars. The lower level of activity in earlier spectral types (e.g., F-type and late A-type stars) is a physical consequence of their short convective turnover times, which prevent them from rotating rapidly enough to ever reach the saturation regime. We demonstrate that a simple model incorporating our derived turnover times versus color can successfully reproduce the observed characteristics of the eROSITA X-ray activity distribution, as measured by the L$_X$/L$_{\text{bol}}$ ratio and {\it Gaia} BP-RP color.

AI.tldr: The study systematically tests the rotation-activity paradigm using TESS rotation periods and eROSITA X-ray data for 14,004 late-type stars, finding that Rossby number-activity relations hold universally, with earlier spectral types unable to reach saturation due to short convective turnover times.
AI.motivation: To systematically test the universal applicability of the rotation-activity paradigm across all late-type stars, which states that activity increases with decreasing rotation period until saturation, with saturation expected at a universal Rossby number across spectral types.
AI.method: Combined TESS short-cadence space photometry to determine rotation periods with eROSITA all-sky survey X-ray measurements for 14,004 late-type stars, representing a sample size increase of more than an order of magnitude compared to previous studies.
AI.result: Convective turnover times derived from the sample closely agree with theoretical computations, supporting universal Rossby number-activity relations. Earlier spectral types (F-type and late A-type) show lower activity due to short convective turnover times preventing them from rotating rapidly enough to reach saturation. A simple model with derived turnover times versus color successfully reproduces observed eROSITA X-ray activity distribution characteristics.
AI.conclusion: The rotation-activity paradigm holds universally for late-type main sequence stars when properly scaled via Rossby number, with spectral type differences in activity levels explained by variations in convective turnover times rather than fundamental breakdown of the paradigm.

title: Dust Absorption towards Supernova Remnant W44
comment: 15 pages, 8 figures, accepted for publication in AJ
summary: Supernova remnants (SNRs) can strongly affect the chemical composition of the interstellar dust. In this paper we investigate to what degree the dust and ices are modified by observing four stars expected to be absorbed by a giant molecular cloud interacting with SNR W44, using medium-resolution spectroscopy in 2-5 $μ$m. Absorption from H2O ice around 3.0 $μ$m and aliphatic hydrocarbon dust around 3.4 $μ$m were detected towards two stars, while probable CO ice at 4.67 $μ$m towards one of them. Millimeter gas-phase CO J = 1-0 lines and three-dimensional dust extinction maps show that the dense molecular gas associated with W44 dominates (> 60%) the total interstellar extinction (A_K ~ 2.6) along these two sightlines. The H2O ice column densities are a factor of 1.5-3 lower than nearby MCs at similar extinctions, possibly because of the destruction of ice by shocks and cosmic rays (CRs) from W44, consistent with the low CO ice abundance relative to H2O (< 12%). One of the sightlines shows an unusually strong 3.4 $μ$m aliphatic hydrocarbon absorption. If the carriers are located in diffuse dust along the sightline, unrelated to W44, its strength is ~ 4 times larger than those typically observed for diffuse dust clouds. Alternatively, the carriers may be enhanced in the W44 environment. We discuss several possible explanations, including shock formation of aliphatic hydrocarbons in diffuse clouds associated with W44, contribution from aliphatic hydrocarbons in shocked and CR-bombarded molecular clouds, and changes in the extinction law due to the SNR interaction.

AI.tldr: Observational study of dust and ice modification by SNR W44 using infrared spectroscopy reveals reduced ice abundances and unusually strong hydrocarbon absorption, suggesting shock/CR processing in the interacting molecular cloud.
AI.motivation: To investigate how supernova remnants modify the chemical composition of interstellar dust and ices by observing stars behind a molecular cloud interacting with SNR W44.
AI.method: Medium-resolution 2-5 μm spectroscopy of four stars behind the W44-interacting molecular cloud, combined with millimeter CO J=1-0 observations and 3D dust extinction mapping to characterize the dust and ice properties.
AI.result: Detected H2O ice (3.0 μm) and aliphatic hydrocarbons (3.4 μm) toward two stars, with probable CO ice (4.67 μm) toward one. H2O ice column densities are 1.5-3× lower than typical molecular clouds at similar extinctions, CO ice abundance is low (<12% relative to H2O), and one sightline shows unusually strong 3.4 μm hydrocarbon absorption (~4× typical diffuse dust values).
AI.conclusion: SNR W44 likely destroys ices via shocks and cosmic rays, explaining reduced ice abundances. The enhanced hydrocarbon absorption suggests either shock formation in diffuse clouds, contribution from shocked/CR-bombarded molecular clouds, or changes in extinction law due to SNR interaction.

title: The puzzling story of flare inactive ultra fast rotating M dwarfs -- III. Investigating X-ray Activity
comment: 14 pages, 9 figures, 5 tables Accepted for publication in MNRAS
summary: According to activity-rotation relations, rapid rotators are expected to show high levels of magnetic activity. However, recent studies with TESS have found Ultra Fast Rotating (UFR) M dwarfs with periods $<1$ d displaying low levels of flaring activity. There have been efforts to explore their magnetic field strengths through spectropolarimetric measurements and to assess the potential for binarity. However, neither could fully explain the lack of observed flaring activity despite their rapid rotation. Another avenue for investigation is to measure their coronal emission for signs of supersaturation: an underluminosity in X-rays observed for some rapidly rotating FGK stars. Therefore, in this study, we utilise X-ray observations from Swift and XMM-Newton of ten M dwarf UFRs with P$_{\rm{rot}}$<1 d to determine their X-ray luminosities. Overall, we do not find evidence for supersaturation amongst our UFR M dwarf stars, instead determining them to be at the saturated level, or perhaps even enhanced. Therefore, supersaturation seems not to be the main driver behind the reduced level of flaring activity observed in these stars, and the mystery behind the magnetic activity of UFR low-mass stars remains. Additionally, we provide an updated analysis on the long term variability within our sample using TESS light curves taken during Cycles 5 and 7. We identify 352 optical flares from our sample with energies between $1.2\times10^{31}$ and $8.7\times10^{34}$ erg. We determine flare rates for each TESS cycle, compare them, identifying variations across a 7 year timespan and attribute this to potential activity cycles.

AI.tldr: Study investigates Ultra Fast Rotating M dwarfs with periods <1 day that show low flaring activity despite rapid rotation, finding they are X-ray saturated or enhanced, not supersaturated, and remain a mystery.
AI.motivation: Ultra Fast Rotating M dwarfs with periods <1 day show unexpectedly low flaring activity despite rapid rotation, contradicting activity-rotation relations. Previous explanations (magnetic field strength, binarity) were insufficient, prompting investigation of potential supersaturation effects seen in rapidly rotating FGK stars.
AI.method: Used X-ray observations from Swift and XMM-Newton for ten M dwarf UFRs with P_rot<1 d to determine X-ray luminosities. Also analyzed TESS light curves from Cycles 5 and 7 to study long-term variability and flare activity.
AI.result: No evidence of supersaturation found - UFR M dwarfs are at saturated or enhanced X-ray levels. Identified 352 optical flares with energies 1.2×10³¹ to 8.7×10³⁴ erg. Found flare rate variations across 7-year timespan, potentially indicating activity cycles.
AI.conclusion: Supersaturation is not the cause of reduced flaring in UFR M dwarfs. The mystery of their magnetic activity persists despite rapid rotation. Observed flare rate variations suggest potential activity cycles in these stars.

title: DustPedia and Local Volume Legacy samples as benchmarks for dust evolution in galaxies
summary:
DustPedia and LVL are two samples representative of the local galaxy population, including in total 1011 unique objects of all morphological types, with a wide range of stellar masses ($M_*$) and star-formation activity, and a spectral coverage from the FUV to the FIR. The purpose of this work is to show that these samples cover two complementary ranges in $M_*$ and morphology, making them an ideal set for constraining the dominant processes in the evolution of the galactic dust content.
  Using the multiwavelength data provided by the two surveys, we fitted the galaxies' spectral energy distribution and estimated their physical properties, in particular the $M_*$, $sM_\mathrm{dust}=M_\mathrm{dust}/M_*$, and sSFR = SFR$/M_*$.
  By combining DustPedia and LVL, we highlight that the $\log_{10}(sM_{\rm dust})$-$\log_{10}(M_*)$ trend is not monotonic. Thanks to a large number of objects across a wide range of $M_*$, we have been able to fit two smoothly-joined linear correlations: a positive for $\log_{10}(M_{*}/$M$_\odot)\lesssim9.5$ (mainly LVL late spirals and irregulars), and a negative one for larger-mass, mainly DustPedia spirals (early types are distinct and more dispersed in the same mass regime). For $\log_{10}(M_{*}/$M$_\odot)>9.5$, we confirm a strong sM_{\rm dust}-sSFR correlation; dwarf galaxies, instead, lie below this trend, with a large scatter of $sM_{\rm dust}$, for -10.5<$\log_{10}$(sSFR/yr$^{-1}$)<-9.0.
  By using chemical evolution models we find that the observed $\log_{10}(sM_{\rm dust})$-$\log_{10}(M_{*})$ and $\log_{10}(sM_{\rm dust})$-$\log_{10}$(sSFR) trends can be interpreted mainly by variations in the initial gas mass budget and the galaxy ages, respectively. Low-mass Sm-Irr galaxies with low $sM_{\rm dust}$ and high sSFR can only be reproduced by the models by assuming high photofragmentation rate of large grains, and/or low grain-growth in clouds.

AI.tldr: Analysis of dust-to-stellar mass ratio (sM_dust) trends across galaxy mass and star formation activity using combined DustPedia and LVL samples reveals a non-monotonic relationship with stellar mass, showing distinct regimes for low-mass and high-mass galaxies, explained by variations in initial gas mass and galaxy age.
AI.motivation: To leverage the complementary DustPedia and LVL galaxy samples covering wide ranges in stellar mass, morphology, and spectral coverage to constrain the dominant processes governing galactic dust content evolution.
AI.method: Combined multiwavelength data from DustPedia and LVL surveys, performed SED fitting to estimate physical properties (stellar mass, dust-to-stellar mass ratio, specific star formation rate), and used chemical evolution models to interpret observed trends.
AI.result: Revealed non-monotonic log(sM_dust)-log(M_*) relationship with two regimes: positive correlation for M_* < 10^9.5 M_sun (LVL late spirals/irregulars) and negative correlation for higher masses (DustPedia spirals). Confirmed strong sM_dust-sSFR correlation for massive galaxies, while dwarf galaxies show large scatter below this trend.
AI.conclusion: The observed dust content trends are primarily explained by variations in initial gas mass budget (for mass dependence) and galaxy ages (for star formation dependence). Low-mass galaxies with low dust content and high star formation require high photofragmentation rates and/or low grain growth in clouds.

title: Chromospheric turbulence as a regulator of stellar wind mass flux
comment: accepted for publication in MNRAS
summary: The mass flux of solar and stellar winds is a key quantity for stellar evolution and space weather, yet its physical regulation mechanism remains an unsolved problem. In particular, conventional Alfvén wave--driven models that self-consistently connect the stellar surface to the stellar wind fail to reproduce the observed scaling between stellar X-ray flux and mass-loss rate, a discrepancy that can be largely attributed to the dissipation of a substantial fraction of the wave energy by chromospheric turbulence. To address this issue, we aim to clarify the role of chromospheric turbulence in regulating the stellar wind mass flux. We perform one-dimensional wave-driven wind simulations, comparing cases with and without chromospheric turbulence suppression to assess its impact on coronal and wind properties. We find that suppressing chromospheric turbulence leads to a systematic increase in the coronal particle flux, and hence the wind mass flux, by up to an order of magnitude, particularly in regions of moderately strong magnetic field. This behavior arises from a combination of changes in the Poynting flux at the coronal base and in the asymptotic wind speed. Furthermore, the model with chromospheric turbulence suppression reproduces the observed empirical scaling between coronal magnetic field strength and mass flux without invoking additional energy input mechanisms such as interchange reconnection. These results identify the chromospheric turbulence as a key factor in regulating stellar wind mass flux and highlight the importance of incorporating its effects in models that connect the stellar surface and the stellar wind.

AI.tldr: Suppressing chromospheric turbulence in wave-driven wind models increases mass flux by up to 10×, reproducing observed scaling between coronal magnetic field and mass flux without extra energy mechanisms.
AI.motivation: The mass flux of solar and stellar winds is poorly understood, with conventional Alfvén wave-driven models failing to reproduce observed scaling between stellar X-ray flux and mass-loss rate, largely due to chromospheric turbulence dissipating wave energy.
AI.method: One-dimensional wave-driven wind simulations comparing cases with and without chromospheric turbulence suppression to assess impact on coronal and wind properties.
AI.result: Suppressing chromospheric turbulence leads to systematic increase in coronal particle flux and wind mass flux by up to an order of magnitude, particularly in moderately strong magnetic field regions, due to changes in Poynting flux at coronal base and asymptotic wind speed.
AI.conclusion: Chromospheric turbulence is a key factor regulating stellar wind mass flux, and models connecting stellar surface to wind must incorporate its effects; suppression reproduces observed empirical scaling without needing additional energy input mechanisms like interchange reconnection.

title: Extended coronal line emission and new clues to a possible dual AGN in the merger J1356+1026
comment: 10 pages, 10 figures, and 1 table. Accepted for publication in A&A
summary: Merging luminous galaxies are ideal laboratories to study some of the most extreme astrophysical phenomena. The local (z=0.1232) obscured quasar J1356+1026 has two nuclei, North and South (J1356N and J1356S), but despite numerous efforts, J1356S had not yet been confirmed as an AGN. Thanks to the superb sensitivity and spatial resolution of the MIRI/MRS instrument on board the JWST, we present new evidence suggesting that J1356S may indeed host an AGN with log L$_{\rm bol}=43.4\pm^{0.6}_{0.5} erg s^{-1}$. This is supported by the detection of strong coronal line emission at this location and by a spectral shape that differs from that of J1356N and those of the narrow-line region (NLR). Aided by the spatially resolved information of MIRI/MRS and VLT/SINFONI, we also find that the high ionization gas, traced by the coronal lines [Ne V]$14.3~μ$m and [Si VI]$1.963 μ$m, has an extension of ~13-15.5 kpc. This is likely a lower limit of the true extension, as suggested by the comparison with optical imaging from HST. {The extended [Ne V] emission can be accounted for by photoionization from the quasar in J1356N in a relatively low density environment, ranging from $\rm n_e\leq 2000-3800 cm^{-3}$ in J1356N and $\rm n_e\leq 600-1200 cm^{-3}$ in J1356S and the NLR, as measured from the [Ne V]$14.3μ$m and $24.3~μ$m lines.

AI.tldr: JWST MIRI/MRS observations reveal J1356S likely hosts an AGN with L_bol~10^43.4 erg/s, supported by coronal line emission, and show extended high-ionization gas spanning 13-15.5 kpc in merging galaxy system J1356+1026.
AI.motivation: To determine whether the southern nucleus (J1356S) in the merging galaxy system J1356+1026 hosts an active galactic nucleus (AGN), which had not been confirmed despite previous studies, and to characterize the extended high-ionization gas in this system using JWST's superior capabilities.
AI.method: Utilized JWST's MIRI/MRS instrument for mid-infrared spectroscopy with high sensitivity and spatial resolution, combined with VLT/SINFONI data for near-infrared coverage and HST optical imaging for comparison, to analyze coronal line emission ([Ne V] and [Si VI]) and spectral properties.
AI.result: J1356S likely hosts an AGN with bolometric luminosity log L_bol = 43.4±0.6/0.5 erg s^-1, evidenced by strong coronal line emission and distinct spectral shape. Extended high-ionization gas spans ~13-15.5 kpc, with electron densities ≤2000-3800 cm^-3 in J1356N and ≤600-1200 cm^-3 in J1356S/NLR, consistent with photoionization from the northern quasar.
AI.conclusion: JWST observations provide compelling evidence for an AGN in J1356S, resolving long-standing uncertainty about this merging system. The extended coronal line emission reveals widespread high-ionization gas likely photoionized by the northern quasar, demonstrating the power of JWST for studying extreme astrophysical environments in galaxy mergers.

title: Searching for Ultracool Dwarfs in Early LSST Data Products
comment: 26 pages, 9 figures, 6 tables; Under review at ApJ, comments welcome!
summary: The Vera C. Rubin Observatory's Legacy Survey of Space and Time (LSST) promises to drastically accelerate the discovery of ultracool dwarfs (UCDs) over the course of its 10-year survey of the Southern Hemisphere. With the official start of LSST imminent, we showcase LSST's capabilities for discovering and characterizing UCDs using early commissioning data (Data Preview 1). The LSST photometric system at this stage remains poorly understood for faint UCDs. Thus, we begin by cross-matching Data Preview 1 against known UCD catalogs. We recover 1 known UCD from the Ultracool Sheet, 17 UCDs from the Dark Energy Survey, and 17 low mass stars from the Gaia Catalog of Nearby Stars. Using these known UCDs alongside recent spectroscopically-confirmed Euclid objects, we select 89 ultracool dwarf candidates in LSST fields, 17 of which are unique to this work. We present our candidates, a photometric temperature estimate, and discuss lessons learned from using early LSST data products. Finally, we turn to the future and predict potential UCD counts in upcoming LSST commissioning data (Data Preview 2), which is expected to be available to the Rubin community in 2026. Using synthetic populations of brown dwarfs, we forecast over 17,000 objects may be discovered and characterized in Data Preview 2. We predict that several hundred known objects and thousands of as-of-yet undiscovered UCDs may be detected in Data Preview 2 fields.

AI.tldr: LSST's early commissioning data (Data Preview 1) demonstrates capability to discover ultracool dwarfs, with 89 candidates identified and predictions of over 17,000 discoveries in upcoming Data Preview 2.
AI.motivation: The Vera C. Rubin Observatory's LSST will revolutionize ultracool dwarf discovery, but the LSST photometric system remains poorly understood for faint UCDs. This work aims to showcase LSST's capabilities using early commissioning data and prepare for future discoveries.
AI.method: Cross-matched Data Preview 1 against known UCD catalogs (Ultracool Sheet, Dark Energy Survey, Gaia Catalog of Nearby Stars), used spectroscopically-confirmed Euclid objects to select 89 ultracool dwarf candidates, and employed synthetic brown dwarf populations to forecast discoveries in upcoming Data Preview 2.
AI.result: Recovered 1 known UCD from Ultracool Sheet, 17 from DES, and 17 low mass stars from Gaia. Selected 89 ultracool dwarf candidates (17 unique to this work) with photometric temperature estimates. Forecasts predict over 17,000 objects may be discovered in Data Preview 2, including several hundred known objects and thousands of undiscovered UCDs.
AI.conclusion: LSST's early commissioning data successfully demonstrates the survey's potential for ultracool dwarf discovery and characterization. The work provides valuable lessons for using early LSST data products and predicts substantial discoveries in upcoming data releases, highlighting LSST's transformative impact on ultracool dwarf astronomy.

title: Expansion kinematics of young clusters. III. The kiloparsec sample
comment: 30 pages, 28 figures, submitted to A&A
summary: We combine Gaia DR3 5-parameter astrometry with calibrated radial velocities for 23 nearby (<1 kpc) young (<60 Myr) clusters, with membership lists from Cantat-Gaudin et al. (2020). We characterise the plane-of-sky structure of the clusters using Q-Parameter and Angular Dispersion Parameter (ADP) methods. We measure plane-of-sky expansion using several methods. We determine plane-of-sky orientations along which expansion is maximised. We also estimate expansion timescales and traceback ages and compare to isochronal ages. We then look for correlations between cluster properties and discuss sample-wide trends. We find that most young clusters are more smoothly structured in their centers where the rate of dynamical interactions is highest, while hierarchical structure can survive in the sparse outskirts for >10 Myr. We also find that the majority of nearby young clusters exhibit clear signatures of expansion in the plane-of-sky, which in many cases is significantly anisotropic, even at ages >30 Myr. We find evidence that older clusters tend to have directions of maximum expansion oriented closer to parallel with the Galactic plane. The high degree of spatial structure and significant expansion anisotropy imply that the majority of these young clusters have formed with significant spatial and kinematic substructure and not as dense, monolithic clusters. Kinematic ages estimated from expansion timescales and on-sky traceback are generally in good agreement with estimates inferred from stellar evolution models for clusters <10 Myr old. However, many clusters with older isochronal ages appear to have significantly younger kinematic ages. We discuss potential reasons for this discrepancy, including a prolonged embedded and/or gravitationally bound phase in the early stages of the clusters.

AI.tldr: Analysis of 23 nearby young clusters using Gaia DR3 astrometry reveals hierarchical structure in outskirts, anisotropic expansion, and discrepancies between kinematic and isochronal ages for older clusters.
AI.motivation: To characterize the spatial structure and expansion patterns of nearby young star clusters, understand their formation mechanisms (monolithic vs. hierarchical), and compare kinematic ages with stellar evolution-based isochronal ages.
AI.method: Combined Gaia DR3 5-parameter astrometry with calibrated radial velocities for 23 clusters (<1 kpc, <60 Myr). Used Q-Parameter and Angular Dispersion Parameter methods for structure analysis, multiple methods for plane-of-sky expansion measurement, and determined expansion timescales and traceback ages.
AI.result: Most young clusters show smooth centers but hierarchical structure in outskirts persisting >10 Myr. Majority exhibit clear anisotropic expansion even at >30 Myr. Older clusters have expansion directions more parallel to Galactic plane. Kinematic ages agree with isochronal ages for <10 Myr clusters, but older clusters show younger kinematic ages.
AI.conclusion: Young clusters form with significant spatial and kinematic substructure rather than as dense monolithic systems. Age discrepancies suggest prolonged embedded/gravitationally bound phases in early cluster evolution, challenging simple expansion models.

title: Neutral Hydrogen in the Shapley Supercluster Core I: Environmental Effects on Gas Content and Galaxy Evolution
comment: 19 pages, 13 figures, Accepted for publication in MNRAS
summary: We study the atomic Hydrogen (HI) content of galaxies in the core of the Shapley Supercluster (SSC) at <z> ~ 0.048, using observations from the MeerKAT Galaxy Cluster Legacy Survey and optical data from the Shapley Supercluster Survey (ShaSS) project. Our sample comprises 169 galaxies with HI detections in the dynamically active region of Abell 3558 and SC1329-313. Following the literature, we classify galaxies into star-forming main sequence (SFMS), transition (TZ), and red sequence (RS) populations, and examine how the HI content varies across these populations. Galaxies on the SFMS exhibit an average HI gas fraction offset of 0.038 dex from the gas fraction main sequence, while TZ and RS populations show depleted HI fractions of -0.034 and -0.211 dex. HI depletion timescales span from 6 to 170 Gyr (SFMS-TZ-RS) confirming increasingly inefficient star formation with quenching. Scaling relations between HI mass and stellar mass in the SSC are generally consistent with field samples. The most direct signature of the dense environment of the SSC is the marked predominance of TZ galaxies, in contrast to what is observed in the field-dominated sample of xGASS, where the population is mostly composed of SFMS galaxies. Moreover, the SFMS and RS populations have similar size, again in contrast with field populations. These results suggest that galaxies in the SSC are undergoing environmental quenching through starvation or strangulation, rather than rapid gas stripping. Despite detectable HI reservoirs, many galaxies exhibit long depletion times, indicating reduced gas accretion and inefficient star formation.

AI.tldr: Study of HI gas content in galaxies within the Shapley Supercluster core reveals environmental quenching via starvation/strangulation, with transition galaxies dominating and long HI depletion times despite detectable gas reservoirs.
AI.motivation: To understand how the dense environment of the Shapley Supercluster affects the atomic hydrogen (HI) content of galaxies and their star formation properties, specifically examining environmental quenching mechanisms in contrast to field galaxies.
AI.method: Used MeerKAT Galaxy Cluster Legacy Survey HI observations and optical data from Shapley Supercluster Survey to analyze 169 HI-detected galaxies in Abell 3558 and SC1329-313 regions. Classified galaxies into star-forming main sequence (SFMS), transition (TZ), and red sequence (RS) populations based on literature criteria, then examined HI gas fractions, depletion timescales, and scaling relations compared to field samples like xGASS.
AI.result: SFMS galaxies show slight HI excess (+0.038 dex), while TZ and RS show depletion (-0.034 and -0.211 dex). HI depletion times increase dramatically from SFMS to RS (6-170 Gyr). SSC shows predominance of TZ galaxies vs. field's SFMS dominance. SFMS and RS galaxies have similar sizes in SSC, unlike field populations. Scaling relations generally consistent with field samples.
AI.conclusion: Galaxies in the Shapley Supercluster are undergoing environmental quenching through starvation/strangulation rather than rapid gas stripping. Despite detectable HI reservoirs, reduced gas accretion leads to long depletion times and inefficient star formation. The dense environment promotes transition to quiescence through gradual gas depletion.

title: Morphological complexity of NGC 628 - a multiwavelength multiscale analysis using the ordinal pattern framework
comment: 20 pages, accepted for publication in ApJ
summary: As statistical systems, galaxies exhibit a rich interplay between organized structure and stochastic fluctuations across a broad range of spatial scales. This duality motivates the need for quantitative frameworks capable of capturing their morphological complexity. The ordinal patterns framework, along with its associated statistical measures: permutation entropy ($H$), disequilibrium ($D_E$), statistical complexity ($C$), and ordinal network node entropy, has recently emerged as a powerful tool for analyzing such complexity in physical systems. We apply this framework in a multiwavelength, multiscale analysis of the galaxy NGC 628, utilizing observations in the near-ultraviolet, near-infrared, mid-infrared, and millimeter bands. Our results reveal a characteristic spatial scale of approximately 200 parsecs, marking the transition from small-scale structures influenced by star formation and stellar feedback to larger-scale morphology governed by the galaxy's dynamics. Furthermore, we find that the $C$ vs. $H$ trajectories for all wavelengths converge toward a common attractor curve, consistent with the behavior of isotropic Gaussian random fields. This convergence suggests a universal statistical behavior in galactic structure at large scales, despite the differing physical processes traced by each wavelength.

AI.tldr: Ordinal patterns framework applied to multiwavelength analysis of NGC 628 reveals 200 pc transition scale between star formation-dominated small scales and dynamics-governed large scales, with universal statistical behavior across wavelengths.
AI.motivation: Galaxies exhibit complex interplay between organized structure and stochastic fluctuations across spatial scales, requiring quantitative frameworks to capture morphological complexity. The ordinal patterns framework has shown promise for analyzing complexity in physical systems.
AI.method: Applied ordinal patterns framework with permutation entropy (H), disequilibrium (D_E), statistical complexity (C), and ordinal network node entropy to multiwavelength, multiscale analysis of galaxy NGC 628 using near-ultraviolet, near-infrared, mid-infrared, and millimeter band observations.
AI.result: Identified characteristic spatial scale of ~200 parsecs marking transition from small-scale structures influenced by star formation and stellar feedback to larger-scale morphology governed by galaxy dynamics. C vs. H trajectories for all wavelengths converge toward common attractor curve consistent with isotropic Gaussian random fields.
AI.conclusion: The convergence of statistical complexity trajectories across wavelengths suggests universal statistical behavior in galactic structure at large scales, despite differing physical processes traced by each wavelength. The ordinal patterns framework effectively captures morphological complexity in galaxies.
